text: Title: Prevalence of feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) in cats at shelters in Portugal Abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are widespread retroviruses causing significant mortality, disease and a reduction in the life expectancy of domestic cats worldwide. This study evaluates the prevalence of FIV and FeLV retroviruses in a sample of domestic cats from shelters located in Portugal and also evaluates the clinical importance associated with their infection. Blood samples were obtained from 326 domestic cats after clinical examination at shelters. Using immunochromatographic assays, we identified the global seroprevalence of FIV and FeLV and determined the association between feline retrovirus infection and cat’s clinical characteristics. Seroprevalence for at least one of the two viruses was of 15.3% (50/326; 95% CI: 11.6–19.7). Of 326 cats, 9.8% (32/326) tested positive for FIV; 2.1% (7/326) for FeLV and 3.4% (11/326) were co-infected by both viruses. Although FIV and co-infections were confirmed in a higher number of adult cats, no significant relationship was confirmed in respect to the other studied factors (sex, breed, hair length and municipality). Based on the absence of previous studies on the presence of retroviruses in shelters in Portugal, our work strongly suggests that continuous surveillance, adherence to veterinary vaccination guidelines and efficient management of the viruses in the domestic cat population is mandatory.
explanation: The abstract contains precise, study-specific data (sample size, percentages, confidence interval) and conventional structure, which is typical of human-authored scientific writing; however, the compact and generic phrasing could also be AI-generated, so uncertainty is mid-range.
